questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic field at a point situated $10 \text{ cm}$ from a magnetic dipole is observed to be $2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$. Calculate the magnetic moment of the dipole if this point is located in the broadside-on position.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the broadside-on (equatorial) position of a short magnetic dipole is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{M}{r^3}$

Given $B = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$ and $r = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$.

Substituting the values into the formula:

$2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{M}{(0.1)^3}$

$2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{M}{10^{-3}}$

$2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-4} \times M$

$M = 2.0 \text{ A m}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic field at a point due to a magnetic dipole is perpendicular to the magnetic axis. Find the angle that the line joining the point with the centre of the dipole makes with the magnetic axis.
questions.solution:
Let the magnetic moment of the dipole be $\vec{M}$ and the position vector of the point be $\vec{r}$.

The magnetic field at a point due to a magnetic dipole is given by:

$\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi r^3} [3(\vec{M} \cdot \hat{r})\hat{r} - \vec{M}]$

Let the magnetic axis be along the x-axis, so $\vec{M} = M \hat{i}$.

The unit vector along the position vector is $\hat{r} = \cos\theta \hat{i} + \sin\theta \hat{j}$, where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{M}$.

Substituting these into the expression for $\vec{B}$:

$\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0 M}{4\pi r^3} [3\cos\theta (\cos\theta \hat{i} + \sin\theta \hat{j}) - \hat{i}]$

$\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0 M}{4\pi r^3} [(3\cos^2\theta - 1)\hat{i} + (3\cos\theta \sin\theta)\hat{j}]$

It is given that the magnetic field is perpendicular to the magnetic axis. Therefore, the component of $\vec{B}$ along the x-axis must be zero.

$3\cos^2\theta - 1 = 0$

$\cos^2\theta = \dfrac{1}{3}$

Using the identity $\sec^2\theta = 1 + \tan^2\theta$:

$\dfrac{1}{\cos^2\theta} = 1 + \tan^2\theta$

$3 = 1 + \tan^2\theta$

$\tan^2\theta = 2$

$\tan\theta = \sqrt{2}$

$\theta = \tan^{-1}(\sqrt{2})$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two long bar magnets are positioned such that their axes coincide and the north pole of the first magnet is located $2.0 \text{ cm}$ away from the south pole of the second. Given that both magnets possess a pole strength of $10 \text{ Am}$, determine the force exerted by one magnet on the other.
questions.solution:
Since the magnets are long, the force between them is predominantly due to their adjacent poles. The effect of the farther poles can be neglected.

The force between two magnetic poles is given by Coulomb's law for magnetism:

$F = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{m_1 m_2}{r^2}$

Given:
Pole strength, $m_1 = m_2 = 10 \text{ Am}$
Distance between the adjacent poles, $r = 2.0 \text{ cm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$
Constant, $\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} = 10^{-7} \text{ N A}^{-2}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$F = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{10 \times 10}{(0.02)^2}$

$F = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{100}{4 \times 10^{-4}}$

$F = 10^{-7} \times 25 \times 10^4$

$F = 25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N} = 0.025 \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A long bar magnet possesses a pole strength of $10 \text{ Am}$. Determine the magnetic field at a point situated on the magnet's axis, at a distance of $5 \text{ cm}$ from its north pole.
questions.solution:
Since the bar magnet is long, the magnetic field at a point near one of its poles is effectively due to that pole alone. The contribution from the other pole is negligible.

The magnetic field $B$ at a distance $r$ from a magnetic pole of strength $m$ is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{m}{r^2}$

Given $m = 10 \text{ Am}$ and $r = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$.

Substituting the values:

$B = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{10}{(0.05)^2}$

$B = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{10}{25 \times 10^{-4}}$

$B = 10^{-7} \times 4000$

$B = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A magnetic dipole having a magnetic moment of $1.44 \text{ A m}^2$ is positioned horizontally such that its north pole points towards the north. Determine the location of the neutral point, given that the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field is $18 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$.
options: At a distance of $20 \text{ cm}$ in the plane bisecting the dipole
At a distance of $40 \text{ cm}$ in the plane bisecting the dipole
At a distance of $20 \text{ cm}$ on the axis of the dipole
At a distance of $40 \text{ cm}$ on the axis of the dipole
questions.solution:
When the north pole of a magnetic dipole points towards the geographic north, its magnetic field on the equatorial line (the plane bisecting the dipole) is directed towards the geographic south. Since the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field $B_H$ is directed towards the geographic north, the neutral points will lie on the equatorial line.

At the neutral point, the magnetic field of the dipole equals $B_H$:

$B_{eq} = B_H$

$\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{M}{d^3} = B_H$

Substituting the given values:

$10^{-7} \times \dfrac{1.44}{d^3} = 18 \times 10^{-6}$

$d^3 = \dfrac{1.44 \times 10^{-7}}{18 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1.44}{180} = 0.008 \text{ m}^3$

$d = \sqrt[3]{0.008} = 0.2 \text{ m} = 20 \text{ cm}$

Thus, the neutral point is located at a distance of $20 \text{ cm}$ in the plane bisecting the dipole.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A magnetic dipole having a magnetic moment of $0.72\sqrt{2} \text{ A m}^2$ is oriented horizontally such that its north pole points towards the east. Determine the position of a neutral point, given that the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field is $18 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$.
options: $20 \text{ cm}$ from the dipole, $\tan^{-1}(\sqrt{2})$ south of east
$20 \text{ cm}$ from the dipole, $\tan^{-1}(\sqrt{2})$ north of east
$20 \text{ cm}$ from the dipole, $\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ south of east
$40 \text{ cm}$ from the dipole, $\tan^{-1}(\sqrt{2})$ south of east
questions.solution:
Let the east direction be along the positive x-axis ($\hat{i}$) and the north direction be along the positive y-axis ($\hat{j}$).

The magnetic moment of the dipole is $\vec{M} = M \hat{i}$, where $M = 0.72\sqrt{2} \text{ A m}^2$.

The horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field is $\vec{B}_H = B_H \hat{j}$, where $B_H = 18 \text{ }\mu\text{T} = 18 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T}$.

At the neutral point, the magnetic field due to the dipole $\vec{B}_d$ must exactly cancel the earth's magnetic field, so $\vec{B}_d = -B_H \hat{j}$.

The magnetic field of a dipole at a position vector $\vec{r}$ is given by:

$\vec{B}_d = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi r^3} [3(\vec{M} \cdot \hat{r})\hat{r} - \vec{M}]$

Let the position vector of the neutral point be $\vec{r} = r(\cos\theta \hat{i} + \sin\theta \hat{j})$, where $\theta$ is the angle measured from the east.

Substituting $\vec{M} = M \hat{i}$ and $\hat{r} = \cos\theta \hat{i} + \sin\theta \hat{j}$:

$\vec{B}_d = \dfrac{\mu_0 M}{4\pi r^3} [3\cos\theta(\cos\theta \hat{i} + \sin\theta \hat{j}) - \hat{i}]$

$\vec{B}_d = \dfrac{\mu_0 M}{4\pi r^3} [(3\cos^2\theta - 1)\hat{i} + (3\sin\theta\cos\theta)\hat{j}]$

For $\vec{B}_d$ to have no x-component:

$3\cos^2\theta - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \cos^2\theta = \dfrac{1}{3}$

This implies $\sin^2\theta = 1 - \cos^2\theta = \dfrac{2}{3}$, so $\tan^2\theta = 2 \Rightarrow \tan\theta = \pm \sqrt{2}$.

Equating the y-component to $-B_H$:

$\dfrac{\mu_0 M}{4\pi r^3} (3\sin\theta\cos\theta) = -B_H$

Since $B_H$ is positive, the product $\sin\theta\cos\theta$ must be negative, which means $\theta$ lies in the fourth quadrant (south of east). Thus, $\tan\theta = -\sqrt{2}$, corresponding to an angle of $\tan^{-1}(\sqrt{2})$ south of east.

Substituting $\cos\theta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ and $\sin\theta = -\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}$ into the y-component equation:

$\dfrac{\mu_0 M}{4\pi r^3} \left[3 \left(-\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)\right] = -B_H$

$\dfrac{\mu_0 M \sqrt{2}}{4\pi r^3} = B_H$

Substituting the given values:

$\dfrac{10^{-7} \times 0.72\sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{2}}{r^3} = 18 \times 10^{-6}$

$\dfrac{1.44 \times 10^{-7}}{r^3} = 18 \times 10^{-6}$

$r^3 = \dfrac{1.44 \times 10^{-7}}{18 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.008 \text{ m}^3$

$r = \sqrt[3]{0.008} = 0.2 \text{ m} = 20 \text{ cm}$

The neutral point is at a distance of $20 \text{ cm}$ from the dipole, at an angle $\tan^{-1}(\sqrt{2})$ south of east.

Answer: $20 \text{ cm}$ from the dipole, $\tan^{-1}(\sqrt{2})$ south of east

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assume the magnetic moment of the dipole at the earth's centre is $8.0 \times 10^{22} \text{ A m}^2$. Determine the magnetic field $B$ at the earth's geomagnetic poles. The radius of the earth is $6400 \text{ km}$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the geomagnetic poles (which lie on the axial line of the earth's magnetic dipole) is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{R^3}$

Given:

$M = 8.0 \times 10^{22} \text{ A m}^2$

$R = 6400 \text{ km} = 6.4 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$

$\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} = 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$

Substituting the values:

$B = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{2 \times 8.0 \times 10^{22}}{(6.4 \times 10^6)^3}$

$B = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{16.0 \times 10^{22}}{262.144 \times 10^{18}}$

$B = 10^{-7} \times 0.061 \times 10^4$

$B = 6.1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose the magnitude of the earth's magnetic field at the magnetic equator is $3.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$. Find its value at the geomagnetic poles of the earth.
questions.solution:
The earth's magnetic field can be approximated as a magnetic dipole.

The magnitude of the magnetic field of a short dipole at a distance $r$ and magnetic latitude $\lambda$ is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 M}{4 \pi r^3} \sqrt{1 + 3 \sin^2 \lambda}$

At the magnetic equator, $\lambda = 0^{\circ}$, so $\sin \lambda = 0$.

$B_{eq} = \dfrac{\mu_0 M}{4 \pi r^3} = 3.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

At the geomagnetic poles, $\lambda = 90^{\circ}$, so $\sin \lambda = 1$.

$B_{pole} = \dfrac{\mu_0 M}{4 \pi r^3} \sqrt{1 + 3(1)^2} = 2 \left( \dfrac{\mu_0 M}{4 \pi r^3} \right)$

$B_{pole} = 2 B_{eq}$

$B_{pole} = 2 \times 3.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T} = 6.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A magnetic needle can freely rotate in a vertical plane that makes an angle of $60^\circ$ with the magnetic meridian. If the needle settles in a direction making an angle of $\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$ with the horizontal, determine the true dip at this location.
questions.solution:
Let the true dip be $\delta$ and the apparent dip be $\delta'$.

The relation between true dip and apparent dip in a plane making an angle $\alpha$ with the magnetic meridian is given by

$\tan \delta' = \dfrac{\tan \delta}{\cos \alpha}$

Given $\delta' = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$, we have $\tan \delta' = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$.

The angle with the magnetic meridian is $\alpha = 60^\circ$, so $\cos \alpha = \dfrac{1}{2}$.

Substituting these values into the formula, we get

$\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{\tan \delta}{1/2}$

$\tan \delta = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \times \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

$\delta = 30^\circ$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a current of $10 \text{ mA}$ is passed through a tangent galvanometer, it exhibits a deflection of $45^\circ$. Determine the number of turns in the coil, given that the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field is $B_H = 3.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$ and the coil's radius is $10 \text{ cm}$.
questions.solution:
The formula for a tangent galvanometer is given by:

$B = B_H \tan \theta$

The magnetic field at the center of the coil is:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2 R}$

Equating the two expressions for $B$:

$\dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2 R} = B_H \tan \theta$

Rearranging to solve for the number of turns $N$:

$N = \dfrac{2 R B_H \tan \theta}{\mu_0 I}$

Substituting the given values $R = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $B_H = 3.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$, $\theta = 45^\circ$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T}\cdot\text{m/A}$, and $I = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}$:

$N = \dfrac{2 \times 0.1 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-5} \times \tan 45^\circ}{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}$

$N = \dfrac{0.72 \times 10^{-5}}{4\pi \times 10^{-9}}$

$N = \dfrac{7200}{4\pi} = \dfrac{1800}{\pi}$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$:

$N \approx \dfrac{1800}{3.14} \approx 573$

The closest integer value among the given options is $570$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a certain position, the needle of a dip circle indicates an apparent dip of $45^\circ$. When the circle is rotated by $90^\circ$, the apparent dip shown is $53^\circ$. Determine the true dip.
questions.solution:
Let the true dip be $\delta$.

The apparent dips in two mutually perpendicular planes are $\delta_1 = 45^\circ$ and $\delta_2 = 53^\circ$.

The relation between true dip and apparent dips is given by:

$\cot^2 \delta = \cot^2 \delta_1 + \cot^2 \delta_2$

Substituting the given values:

$\cot^2 \delta = \cot^2 45^\circ + \cot^2 53^\circ$

$\cot^2 \delta = (1)^2 + \left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)^2$

$\cot^2 \delta = 1 + \dfrac{9}{16} = \dfrac{25}{16}$

$\cot \delta = \dfrac{5}{4}$

$\tan \delta = \dfrac{4}{5} = 0.8$

$\delta = \tan^{-1}(0.8) \approx 39^\circ$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A moving-coil galvanometer contains a $50$-turn coil with dimensions $2 \text{ cm} \times 2 \text{ cm}$. It is suspended between magnetic poles that produce a magnetic field of $0.5 \text{ T}$. Determine the torque exerted on the coil by the magnetic field when a current of $20 \text{ mA}$ flows through it.
questions.solution:
Given:

$N = 50$

$A = 2 \text{ cm} \times 2 \text{ cm} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

$B = 0.5 \text{ T}$

$I = 20 \text{ mA} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}$

The torque on the coil in a moving-coil galvanometer with a radial magnetic field is given by:

$\tau = NIAB$

Substituting the given values:

$\tau = 50 \times (20 \times 10^{-3}) \times (4 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.5$

$\tau = 1000 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}$

$\tau = 1 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When placed at a separation of $10 \text{ cm}$ from the needle, a short magnet causes a deflection of $37^\circ$ in a deflection magnetometer in the Tan-A position. Determine the ratio of the magnet's magnetic moment to the earth's horizontal magnetic field.
questions.solution:
In the Tan-A position, the magnetic field $B$ due to a short magnet on its axial line is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{d^3}$

The deflection $\theta$ is given by the relation:

$B = B_H \tan\theta$

Equating the two expressions for $B$:

$\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{d^3} = B_H \tan\theta$

Rearranging to find the ratio $\dfrac{M}{B_H}$:

$\dfrac{M}{B_H} = \dfrac{d^3 \tan\theta}{2 \left( \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \right)}$

Substituting $d = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $\theta = 37^\circ$, and $\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} = 10^{-7} \text{ T}\cdot\text{m/A}$:

$\dfrac{M}{B_H} = \dfrac{(0.1)^3 \times \tan 37^\circ}{2 \times 10^{-7}}$

Using $\tan 37^\circ = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75$:

$\dfrac{M}{B_H} = \dfrac{10^{-3} \times 0.75}{2 \times 10^{-7}}$

$\dfrac{M}{B_H} = \dfrac{0.75}{2} \times 10^4$

$\dfrac{M}{B_H} = 0.375 \times 10000 = 3750 \text{ A}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic field at a point situated $10 \text{ cm}$ from a magnetic dipole is observed to be $2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$. Calculate the magnetic moment of the dipole if this point is located in the end-on position.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the end-on (axial) position of a short magnetic dipole is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{r^3}$

Given $B = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$ and $r = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$.

Substituting the values into the formula:

$2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{2M}{(0.1)^3}$

$2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{2M}{10^{-3}}$

$2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-4} \times 2M$

$2M = 2.0$

$M = 1.0 \text{ A m}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When positioned $10 \text{ cm}$ from the needle, a short magnet causes a $37^\circ$ deflection in a deflection magnetometer set in the Tan-A position. At what separation must the magnet be placed to produce a $37^\circ$ deflection of the needle if the magnetometer is instead used in the Tan-B position with the same magnet?
questions.solution:
In the Tan-A position, the magnetic field at the needle due to a short magnet at distance $d_1$ is given by:

$B_A = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{d_1^3}$

The deflection $\theta$ is given by $B_A = B_H \tan \theta$.

In the Tan-B position, the magnetic field at the needle due to the same short magnet at distance $d_2$ is given by:

$B_B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{M}{d_2^3}$

The deflection $\theta$ is given by $B_B = B_H \tan \theta$.

Since the deflection is $37^\circ$ in both cases, the magnetic fields must be equal:

$B_A = B_B$

$\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{d_1^3} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{M}{d_2^3}$

$\dfrac{2}{d_1^3} = \dfrac{1}{d_2^3}$

$d_2^3 = \dfrac{d_1^3}{2}$

$d_2 = \dfrac{d_1}{2^{1/3}}$

Given $d_1 = 10 \text{ cm}$ and using $2^{1/3} \approx 1.26$:

$d_2 = \dfrac{10}{1.26} \approx 7.9 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A deflection magnetometer is arranged with its arms oriented in the north–south direction. At what distance and in which orientation must a short magnet having $M/B_H = 40 \text{ A m}^2 \text{ T}^{-1}$ be placed on the arms so that the compass needle can rest in any arbitrary position?
options: $1.6 \text{ cm}$ from the needle, with its north pole pointing towards north
$1.6 \text{ cm}$ from the needle, with its north pole pointing towards south
$2.0 \text{ cm}$ from the needle, with its north pole pointing towards south
$2.0 \text{ cm}$ from the needle, with its north pole pointing towards north
questions.solution:
For the compass needle to rest in any arbitrary position, the net magnetic field at the center of the magnetometer must be zero. This means the magnetic field produced by the short magnet ($\vec{B}$) must be equal and opposite to the horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field ($\vec{B}_H$).

Since $\vec{B}_H$ is directed towards the geographic North, the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ due to the magnet at the location of the needle must be directed towards the geographic South.

The arms of the deflection magnetometer are along the North-South direction. The magnet is placed on the arms, which means the compass needle lies on the axial line of the short magnet.

The magnetic field on the axial line of a short magnet is given by:
$\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2\vec{M}}{d^3}$

The direction of the magnetic field on the axial line is the same as the direction of the magnetic moment $\vec{M}$ (which points from the South pole to the North pole of the magnet). For $\vec{B}$ to point towards the South, $\vec{M}$ must also point towards the South. Thus, the North pole of the magnet must point towards the South.

Equating the magnitudes of the fields:
$B_H = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{d^3}$

Rearranging the formula to solve for $d^3$:
$d^3 = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \times 2 \times \left(\dfrac{M}{B_H}\right)$

Substituting the given values $\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} = 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$ and $\dfrac{M}{B_H} = 40 \text{ A m}^2 \text{ T}^{-1}$:
$d^3 = 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 40$
$d^3 = 80 \times 10^{-7} = 8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$

Taking the cube root:
$d = 2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 2.0 \text{ cm}$

Therefore, the magnet must be placed at a distance of $2.0 \text{ cm}$ from the needle with its North pole pointing towards the South.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bar magnet is $8 \text{ cm}$ long. At a distance of $3 \text{ cm}$ from its centre in the broadside-on position, the magnetic field is measured to be $4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T}$. Determine the pole strength of this magnet.
questions.solution:
Given:
Length of the magnet, $2l = 8 \text{ cm} \Rightarrow l = 4 \text{ cm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
Distance from the centre, $d = 3 \text{ cm} = 0.03 \text{ m}$
Magnetic field, $B = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T}$

The magnetic field at a distance $d$ on the equatorial line (broadside-on position) of a bar magnet is given by:
$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{M}{(d^2 + l^2)^{3/2}}$
where $M = m \times 2l$ is the magnetic moment and $m$ is the pole strength.

Substituting the given values:
$4 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{m \times 0.08}{((0.03)^2 + (0.04)^2)^{3/2}}$

$4 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{m \times 0.08}{(0.0009 + 0.0016)^{3/2}}$

$4 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{m \times 0.08}{(0.0025)^{3/2}}$

$4 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{m \times 0.08}{(0.05)^3}$

$4 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{m \times 0.08}{1.25 \times 10^{-4}}$

$m \times 0.08 \times 10^{-7} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$

$m \times 8 \times 10^{-9} = 5 \times 10^{-10}$

$m = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-10}}{8 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{5}{80} = 0.0625 \text{ A m}$

$m = 6.25 \times 10^{-2} \text{ A m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an oscillation magnetometer, a bar magnet requires $\pi/10$ second to complete one oscillation. The moment of inertia of the magnet about its axis of rotation is $1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg m}^2$, and the earth's horizontal magnetic field is $30 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$. Determine the magnetic moment of the magnet.
questions.solution:
The time period of oscillation of a bar magnet in a magnetic field is given by:

$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{MB_H}}$

Squaring both sides, we get:

$T^2 = \dfrac{4\pi^2 I}{MB_H}$

Rearranging for the magnetic moment $M$:

$M = \dfrac{4\pi^2 I}{T^2 B_H}$

Substituting the given values $T = \dfrac{\pi}{10} \text{ s}$, $I = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg m}^2$, and $B_H = 30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T}$:

$M = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4}}{\left(\dfrac{\pi}{10}\right)^2 \times 30 \times 10^{-6}}$

$M = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4}}{\dfrac{\pi^2}{100} \times 30 \times 10^{-6}}$

$M = \dfrac{4 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 100}{30 \times 10^{-6}}$

$M = \dfrac{4.8 \times 10^{-2}}{30 \times 10^{-6}}$

$M = 1600 \text{ A m}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an oscillation magnetometer, a combination of two bar magnets completes $10$ oscillations per second when their like poles are tied together, and $2$ oscillations per second when their unlike poles are tied together. Determine the ratio of the magnetic moments of the two magnets, neglecting any induced magnetism.
questions.solution:
The frequency of oscillation in an oscillation magnetometer is given by $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{MB}{I}}$, which implies $f \propto \sqrt{M}$ for a constant moment of inertia $I$ and magnetic field $B$.

When like poles are tied together, the effective magnetic moment is $M_1 + M_2$. The frequency is $f_1 = 10$.

When unlike poles are tied together, the effective magnetic moment is $M_1 - M_2$. The frequency is $f_2 = 2$.

Taking the ratio of the frequencies:

$\dfrac{f_1}{f_2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{M_1 + M_2}{M_1 - M_2}}$

Substituting the given values:

$\dfrac{10}{2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{M_1 + M_2}{M_1 - M_2}}$

$5 = \sqrt{\dfrac{M_1 + M_2}{M_1 - M_2}}$

Squaring both sides:

$25 = \dfrac{M_1 + M_2}{M_1 - M_2}$

$25(M_1 - M_2) = M_1 + M_2$

$25M_1 - 25M_2 = M_1 + M_2$

$24M_1 = 26M_2$

$\dfrac{M_1}{M_2} = \dfrac{26}{24} = \dfrac{13}{12}$

The ratio of the magnetic moments is $13 : 12$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A short magnet in an oscillation magnetometer oscillates with a time period of $0.10 \text{ s}$ at a location where the earth's horizontal magnetic field is $24 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$. A downward current of $18 \text{ A}$ is set up in a vertical wire positioned $20 \text{ cm}$ east of the magnet. Determine the new time period.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by the vertical wire at the position of the magnet is given by:

$B_{\text{wire}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$

Substituting the given values:

$B_{\text{wire}} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 18}{2\pi \times 0.20} = 18 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T} = 18 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

By the right-hand grip rule, for a downward current in a wire located east of the magnet, the magnetic field at the magnet's position points North. Since the Earth's horizontal magnetic field $B_H$ also points North, the two magnetic fields add up.

The net magnetic field is:

$B_{\text{net}} = B_H + B_{\text{wire}} = 24 \text{ }\mu\text{T} + 18 \text{ }\mu\text{T} = 42 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

The time period of an oscillation magnetometer is given by $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{MB}}$, which implies $T \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{B}}$.

Therefore, the new time period $T_2$ is:

$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{B_1}{B_2}}$

$T_2 = 0.10 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{24}{42}} = 0.10 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{4}{7}}$

$T_2 \approx 0.10 \times 0.756 = 0.0756 \text{ s} \approx 0.076 \text{ s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an oscillation magnetometer, a bar magnet completes $40$ oscillations per minute. A completely demagnetized identical magnet is then placed over the first magnet in the magnetometer. Determine the time required for this combination to complete $40$ oscillations, neglecting any induced magnetism.
questions.solution:
The time period of oscillation of a bar magnet in a uniform magnetic field is given by $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{MB}}$, where $I$ is the moment of inertia and $M$ is the magnetic moment.

Let the initial moment of inertia be $I$ and the magnetic moment be $M$. The time taken for $40$ oscillations is $t_1 = 1$ minute.

When an identical demagnetized magnet is placed over the first one, the total moment of inertia becomes $I' = I + I = 2I$. The magnetic moment remains $M' = M$ since the second magnet is demagnetized and induced magnetism is neglected.

The new time period is $T' = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2I}{MB}} = \sqrt{2} T$.

Since the time required for a fixed number of oscillations is directly proportional to the time period, the new time for $40$ oscillations is $t_2 = \sqrt{2} t_1$.

Substituting $t_1 = 1$ minute, we get $t_2 = \sqrt{2}$ minutes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an oscillation magnetometer, a short magnet completes $40$ oscillations per minute at a location where the earth's horizontal magnetic field is $25 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$. A second short magnet, possessing a magnetic moment of $1.6 \text{ A m}^2$, is positioned $20 \text{ cm}$ east of the oscillating magnet. Calculate the new frequency of oscillation assuming the second magnet has its north pole oriented towards the north.
questions.solution:
The initial frequency of the oscillating magnet is $f_1 = 40 \text{ oscillations/min}$ in the earth's horizontal magnetic field $B_H = 25 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$.

The second magnet is placed $20 \text{ cm}$ east of the oscillating magnet, with its north pole pointing north. This means its magnetic moment $\vec{M}$ points north, and the oscillating magnet lies on its equatorial line.

The magnetic field produced by the second magnet at the position of the oscillating magnet is given by the equatorial field formula:

$B_{eq} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{M}{d^3}$

Substituting the given values ($M = 1.6 \text{ A m}^2$ and $d = 0.2 \text{ m}$):

$B_{eq} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{1.6}{(0.2)^3} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{1.6}{0.008} = 200 \times 10^{-7} \text{ T} = 20 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

On the equatorial line, the magnetic field direction is opposite to the magnetic moment. Since $\vec{M}$ points north, $B_{eq}$ points south.

The net magnetic field at the location of the oscillating magnet is the vector sum of $B_H$ (which points north) and $B_{eq}$ (which points south):

$B_{net} = B_H - B_{eq} = 25 \text{ }\mu\text{T} - 20 \text{ }\mu\text{T} = 5 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

The frequency of oscillation of a magnetometer is given by $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{MB}{I}}$, which implies $f \propto \sqrt{B}$.

$\dfrac{f_2}{f_1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{B_{net}}{B_H}}$

$f_2 = 40 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{5}{25}} = 40 \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}} = \dfrac{40}{2.236} \approx 17.88 \text{ oscillations/min}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, the new frequency is $18 \text{ oscillations/min}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an oscillation magnetometer, a short magnet completes $40$ oscillations per minute at a location where the earth's horizontal magnetic field is $25 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$. A second short magnet, possessing a magnetic moment of $1.6 \text{ A m}^2$, is positioned $20 \text{ cm}$ east of the oscillating magnet. Calculate the new frequency of oscillation assuming the second magnet has its north pole oriented towards the south.
questions.solution:
The initial frequency of the oscillation magnetometer is given by:

$f_1 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{M' B_H}{I}}$

where $B_H = 25 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$ is the Earth's horizontal magnetic field, which points towards the North.

A second short magnet is placed $20 \text{ cm}$ ($0.2 \text{ m}$) East of the oscillating magnet. Since its North pole is oriented towards the South, its magnetic moment $\vec{M}$ points towards the South (as magnetic moment is directed from the South pole to the North pole).

The oscillating magnet lies on the equatorial line of this second magnet. The magnetic field $\vec{B}$ produced by a short magnet at an equatorial point is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{M}{d^3}$

Substituting the given values:

$B = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{1.6}{(0.2)^3} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{1.6}{0.008} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T} = 20 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

At an equatorial point, the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is directed opposite to the magnetic moment $\vec{M}$. Since $\vec{M}$ points South, $\vec{B}$ points North.

Because both the Earth's horizontal magnetic field $B_H$ and the field $B$ due to the second magnet point North, the net magnetic field $B_{net}$ is their sum:

$B_{net} = B_H + B = 25 \text{ }\mu\text{T} + 20 \text{ }\mu\text{T} = 45 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

The frequency of oscillation is directly proportional to the square root of the net magnetic field ($f \propto \sqrt{B_{net}}$). Therefore, the new frequency $f_2$ is:

$f_2 = f_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{B_{net}}{B_H}}$

$f_2 = 40 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{45}{25}} = 40 \times \dfrac{3\sqrt{5}}{5} = 24\sqrt{5}$

Using $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$:

$f_2 \approx 24 \times 2.236 \approx 53.66 \text{ oscillations/min}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, the new frequency is $54 \text{ oscillations/min}$.

Answer: $54 \text{ oscillations/min}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A magnetic dipole having a magnetic moment of $0.72 \text{ A m}^2$ is placed horizontally such that its north pole points towards south. Determine the position of the neutral points, given that the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field is $18 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$.
options: At a distance of $15.9 \text{ cm}$ on the equatorial line
At a distance of $20 \text{ cm}$ on the axial line
At a distance of $20 \text{ cm}$ on the equatorial line
At a distance of $15.9 \text{ cm}$ on the axial line
questions.solution:
When the north pole of a magnet points towards the geographic south, its magnetic moment $\vec{M}$ (directed from the south pole to the north pole of the magnet) points towards the geographic south.

The earth's horizontal magnetic field $B_H$ points towards the geographic north.

On the axial line, the magnetic field of the dipole $B_{axial}$ is in the direction of $\vec{M}$, which is towards the geographic south. Thus, $B_{axial}$ and $B_H$ are in opposite directions, and neutral points are obtained on the axial line.

At the neutral point, the magnetic field of the dipole equals the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field:

$B_{axial} = B_H$

$\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{d^3} = B_H$

Substituting the given values, $M = 0.72 \text{ A m}^2$ and $B_H = 18 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T}$:

$10^{-7} \times \dfrac{2 \times 0.72}{d^3} = 18 \times 10^{-6}$

$\dfrac{1.44 \times 10^{-7}}{d^3} = 18 \times 10^{-6}$

$d^3 = \dfrac{1.44 \times 10^{-7}}{18 \times 10^{-6}}$

$d^3 = \dfrac{1.44}{180} = 0.008 \text{ m}^3$

$d = \sqrt[3]{0.008} \text{ m} = 0.2 \text{ m} = 20 \text{ cm}$

The neutral points are located at a distance of $20 \text{ cm}$ on the axial line.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At a location where the angle of dip is $60^\circ$, the earth's magnetic field has a horizontal component of $26 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$. Determine the vertical component as well as the magnitude of the magnetic field.
questions.solution:
Given angle of dip, $\delta = 60^\circ$

Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field, $B_H = 26 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

The vertical component $B_V$ is given by:

$B_V = B_H \tan\delta$

$B_V = 26 \tan 60^\circ = 26 \sqrt{3} \approx 26 \times 1.732 = 45.03 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \approx 45 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

The magnitude of the earth's magnetic field $B$ is given by:

$B = \dfrac{B_H}{\cos\delta}$

$B = \dfrac{26}{\cos 60^\circ} = \dfrac{26}{1/2} = 52 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

Thus, the vertical component is $45 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$ and the magnitude of the magnetic field is $52 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Several equipotential surfaces of a magnetic scalar potential are depicted in the figure. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field $B$ at a point within this region.
questions.solution:
The relationship between the magnetic field $B$ and the magnetic scalar potential $V$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta r_{\perp}}$, where $\Delta r_{\perp}$ is the perpendicular distance between the equipotential surfaces.

From the given figure, the distance between two adjacent equipotential lines along the x-axis is:
$\Delta x = 20 \text{ cm} - 10 \text{ cm} = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$

The equipotential lines are inclined at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the x-axis. The perpendicular distance $\Delta r_{\perp}$ between the adjacent equipotential lines is:
$\Delta r_{\perp} = \Delta x \sin(30^{\circ}) = 0.1 \times \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.05 \text{ m}$

The difference in magnetic scalar potential between these adjacent lines is:
$\Delta V = 0.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T m} - 0.1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T m} = 0.1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T m}$

The magnitude of the magnetic field is:
$B = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta r_{\perp}} = \dfrac{0.1 \times 10^{-4}}{0.05} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform magnetic field of $0.20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$ is present in space. Determine the change in the magnetic scalar potential as one travels through $50 \text{ cm}$ along the field.
questions.solution:
The magnetic scalar potential $V_m$ is related to the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ by the relation $\vec{B} = -\nabla V_m$.

The change in magnetic scalar potential $dV_m$ over a small displacement $d\vec{l}$ is given by $dV_m = -\vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l}$.

When moving along the direction of the magnetic field, the angle between $\vec{B}$ and $d\vec{l}$ is $0^{\circ}$, so $\vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = B dl$.

For a uniform magnetic field, the change in magnetic scalar potential over a distance $\Delta l$ is:

$\Delta V_m = -B \Delta l$

Given $B = 0.20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$ and $\Delta l = 50 \text{ cm} = 0.50 \text{ m}$, we get:

$\Delta V_m = -(0.20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}) \times (0.50 \text{ m}) = -0.10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T m}$

The negative sign indicates that the magnetic scalar potential decreases along the direction of the magnetic field. Therefore, it decreases by $0.10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T m}$.
